outcome.headline: Agent's first added test asserts on internal `self.cmd.addr` which the gold fix never modifies, so that test fails on both base and gold (no FAIL→PASS), breaking the SWT-bench gate.
outcome.what_verifier_checked: SWT-bench gate: model-added tests must FAIL on base code and PASS after applying the gold runserver.py patch (which renames `0` to `0.0.0.0` only in the displayed `addr` field, not in `self.addr`).
outcome.what_agent_produced: Two tests in tests/admin_scripts/tests.py::ManageRunserver: `test_runner_zero_addr` (asserts `self.cmd.addr == '0.0.0.0'`) and `test_runner_zero_addr_output` (asserts `0.0.0.0:8000` appears in startup output). The first would still fail on gold because the gold patch only rewrites the local display `addr`, leaving `self.addr` as `'0'`.

failure_modes.name: Over-specified test asserts on internal state the canonical fix does not change
failure_modes.description: The agent inferred the fix must convert '0' to '0.0.0.0' during address parsing (changing self.addr), but the canonical Django patch only constructs a local display variable inside inner_run() and leaves self.addr untouched. Asserting self.cmd.addr == '0.0.0.0' therefore fails under the gold patch as well, violating SWT-Bench's fail-to-pass requirement and preventing the parser from emitting PASSED.
failure_modes.evidence_quote:
def test_runner_zero_addr(self):
        """'0' is a shorthand for '0.0.0.0'."""
        call_command(self.cmd, addrport="0:8000")
        self.assertServerSettings("0.0.0.0", "8000")

counterfactual.X: In step 32, write only the test_runner_zero_addr_output test (which checks the startup-message string), and omit test_runner_zero_addr that asserts on self.cmd.addr.
counterfactual.Y: The canonical fix changes only the local display variable in inner_run, leaving self.addr == '0'; an assertion on self.cmd.addr therefore fails on the gold code and breaks the SWT-Bench F2P criterion.

failure_modes.name: Agent never simulated the gold fix to validate its tests
failure_modes.description: The agent stopped after confirming the tests fail on the unpatched repo, never sketching what the upstream fix would look like to confirm both tests would pass after it. A 30-second mental check would have shown the `self.addr` assertion is too strict.
failure_modes.evidence_quote: Both tests fail as expected — they correctly demonstrate the bug.

failure_modes.name: Test asserts on unchanged internal attribute
failure_modes.description: The first test asserts self.cmd.addr == '0.0.0.0', but the gold patch introduces a new LOCAL variable addr (normalized to '0.0.0.0' only for the display string) and never reassigns self.addr. The test therefore fails on the gold code as well, breaking the Fail-to-Pass contract.

counterfactual.X: Replace the self.cmd.addr/self.assertServerSettings('0.0.0.0', '8000') assertion in test_runner_zero_addr with an output-message check (or remove it), since test_runner_zero_addr_output already exercises the user-visible bug correctly.
counterfactual.Y: Test #2 alone is F2P (fails on base, passes on gold), satisfying the SWTBench parser and yielding reward 1.0.

failure_modes.name: Test over-asserts on internal state the fix does not touch
failure_modes.description: The agent assumed the canonical fix would set `self.addr = '0.0.0.0'`, but the upstream Django fix only rewrites a local display variable. The added `test_runner_zero_addr` therefore stays FAIL after the gold patch, breaking the FAIL→PASS transition SWT-bench requires.

counterfactual.X: Drop `test_runner_zero_addr` (or weaken it to inspect the rendered output) and keep only `test_runner_zero_addr_output`, which checks the user-visible 'Starting development server at' line that the gold fix actually changes.
counterfactual.Y: With only an output-level assertion, the model test transitions FAIL→PASS under the gold patch, satisfying the SWT-bench parser and yielding reward 1.0.

failure_modes.name: Validation only checked failure-on-base, never simulated pass-on-gold
failure_modes.description: After writing the tests the agent only verified that they fail on the unfixed code and concluded 'tests work correctly'. It never mentally or mechanically simulated the gold patch (which it could have inferred from the issue: change only the displayed string), so the P2P direction required by SWT-bench was never validated.
failure_modes.evidence_quote: Both tests fail as expected — they demonstrate the bug where `0:8000` results in `addr="0"` and the output shows `http://0:8000/` instead of `http://0.0.0.0:8000/`.

failure_modes.name: Treated local fails as done
failure_modes.description: After running the new tests against unfixed code and seeing AssertionError 0 != 0.0.0.0, the agent concluded the task was complete because failures demonstrate the bug, without validating that all added tests pass when the gold production patch is applied (the SWTBench pass criterion).
failure_modes.evidence_quote: Both tests fail as expected — they demonstrate the bug where `0:8000` results in `addr="0"`

failure_modes.name: Misidentified bug location as parser instead of display formatter
failure_modes.description: The agent's reasoning concluded the regex/parsing path was the bug site ('regex matches it as a FQDN ... self.addr is set to "0"'), and built a test that probes that path. The user's issue is purely about the displayed startup message, and the canonical fix is in inner_run's message construction, not in parsing.
failure_modes.evidence_quote: When you pass `0:8000` to runserver, the regex matches it as a FQDN (hostname) \"0\" rather than as an IPv4 address. This means `self.addr` is set to \"0\" instead of \"0.0.0.0\". The output then shows `http://0:8000/` instead of `http://0.0.0.0:8000/`.

failure_modes.name: Wrong addr attribute assertion
failure_modes.description: At step 31 the agent added test_runner_zero_addr requiring self.cmd.addr to be 0.0.0.0 after addrport='0:8000'. The gold fix in task/tests/test.sh only maps self.addr=='0' to a local display variable in inner_run; self.addr stays '0', so this test fails even with the gold patch.

counterfactual.X: Add only an output-focused test (e.g. assertIn 'Starting development server at http://0.0.0.0:8000/' in stdout) matching solution/solve.sh, without asserting self.cmd.addr
counterfactual.Y: Added test_runner_zero_addr asserting self.assertServerSettings('0.0.0.0', '8000') on the command's addr attribute

failure_modes.name: Misread failing tests as success
failure_modes.description: After seeing the new tests fail on unfixed code, the agent concluded the test suite was correct and stopped without running tests alongside the gold patch, missing that one test would still fail after the canonical fix.
failure_modes.evidence_quote: The tests are working correctly. Both new tests fail as expected (since the bug hasn't been fixed yet), and existing tests still pass.

failure_modes.name: Never validated tests against the gold (expected) fix
failure_modes.description: Agent only ran the new tests against base code and confirmed they fail; never applied a candidate fix or reasoned about what the gold patch actually changes. Had it done so, it would have noticed self.addr is not normalized by the fix and corrected the assertion.
failure_modes.evidence_quote: Both tests fail as expected \u2014 they correctly demonstrate the bug.

failure_modes.name: Repeated re-analysis loops
failure_modes.description: Before writing tests, the agent restated the same root-cause analysis across many steps (12, 15, 20, 24, 30) and burned steps on failed local test invocations with wrong runners/settings (32, 35, 38, 42) instead of converging on SWTBench-compatible tests.
failure_modes.evidence_quote: Now I understand the issue. When `runserver 0:8000` is used

reward_hacking.evidence: Agent wrote genuine pytest-style assertions against real Django runserver behavior; no manipulation of reward.txt, parser, or test harness.

task_quality.structural_hackability_notes: Verifier runs the model patch against both base and gold code via separate scripts and only awards reward when the parser detects a PASSED FAIL→PASS pattern; not trivially gameable from inside /testbed.

notes_for_aggregation: SWT-bench failure pattern: model writes one test that asserts on private/internal attributes a real upstream patch leaves untouched, so the test is FAIL→FAIL under gold and the whole submission is rejected even when a second, output-level test would have been valid.

aggregated_from.note: Union of all judge×round audits, deduped by (A,B,C,D). Step indices remapped from the audits' 1-indexing to the viewer's 0-indexing.
